coredata.dc:title: A facile solvothermal method synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/BiOX (X=Br, Cl) hybrid material for enhanced visible-light photoactivity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenjun
Fu, Jie
Wang, Yuan
Zhang, Xiaoxiong
Li, Jinlin
coredata.dc:description: An efficient photocatalyst consisting of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) dispersed on the surface of the three-dimensional hierarchical bismuth oxyhalide (BiOX, X = Br, Cl) was synthesized via a simple solvothermal method. The catalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS), and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). The N-GQDs play an important role in the improvement of photocatalytic performance because it significantly increases BiOX absorption of visible light and effectively traps photogenerated electrons to accelerate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The superior activity of N-GQDs/BiOX was exhibited to the organic contaminant degradation under visible light. The 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl exhibited strong catalytic activity, in which RhB was almost completely removed after irradiation for 60 and 75 min, respectively. In our work, we provide a strategy that for improving the photocatalytic activity of BiOX with semiconductor quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N–GQDs
BiOX
Solvothermal method
Photocatalytic
Visible light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of as-prepared samples: (a) BiOBr and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr; (b) BiOCl and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl.
SEM images of prepared photocatalysts (a–b) BiOBr; (c–d) BiOCl; (e) 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr; (f) 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl.
XPS spectra of the 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOX: (a) and (b) survey XPS spectrum; (c) Bi 4f ; (d) O 1 s; (e) Br 3d (f) Cl 2p; and (g) C 1 s.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the BiOX, BiOCl, 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl.
The (αhν) 1/2 vs photon energy(hν) for BiOBr, BiOCl, 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl.
Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of (a) BiOBr and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr; (b) BiOCl and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl samples (λex = 315 nm) and (c) N-GQDs.
(a) and (c) Visible light absorption spectra of RhB; (b) and (d) photocatalytic degradation rate of RhB.
Activity capture experiment (a) 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr and (b) 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl.
The mechanism diagram of N-GQDs/BiOX of degradation of RhB (a) 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr and (b) 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl.

head.ce:title: A facile solvothermal method synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/BiOX (X=Br, Cl) hybrid material for enhanced visible-light photoactivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300130, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient photocatalyst consisting of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) dispersed on the surface of the three-dimensional hierarchical bismuth oxyhalide (BiOX, X = Br, Cl) was synthesized via a simple solvothermal method. The catalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS), and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). The N-GQDs play an important role in the improvement of photocatalytic performance because it significantly increases BiOX absorption of visible light and effectively traps photogenerated electrons to accelerate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The superior activity of N-GQDs/BiOX was exhibited to the organic contaminant degradation under visible light. The 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl exhibited strong catalytic activity, in which RhB was almost completely removed after irradiation for 60 and 75 min, respectively. In our work, we provide a strategy that for improving the photocatalytic activity of BiOX with semiconductor quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: BiOX (X = Br, Cl) is a special two-dimensional layered structure semiconductor material consisting of a double X-ion layer and [Bi2O2]2+ alternately arranged and a double-layered X atomic layer formed by X atoms through non-bonding bonding [1]. Stability and photoactivity are emerged due to the special layered structure of BiOX [2,3]. However, the photocatalytic performance of BiOX materials is still limited by the narrow light absorption range and high photocarrier recombination rate. In order to suppress the recombination of carriers and accelerate its separation, researchers have conducted quantum dots (QDs) decorating of BiOX to improve its photoactivity, such as CQDs/BiOX [4], N-CDs/BiOBr [5], N-GQDs/BiOBr [6], Ag QDs/BiOBr [7] and QDs-Cu2O/BiOBr [8]. Therefore, it remains a great challenge to find an appropriate QDs modified BiOX for reducing the recombination of photogenerated carriers.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a kind of graphene derivatives, are the zero-dimensional nanomaterial and prevents excellent chemical properties [9]. Due to the distinguished light absorption range, photoelectric conversion ability, and good fluorescence capability, GQDs can be applied to various fields such as photocatalysis [10], photoelectric conversion [11] and ion detection [12]. Based on the study of GQDs, discovers that non-metal nitrogen is doped into the lattice of GQDs, which not only broadens the absorption range of sunlight, but also enhances its fluorescence performance [13]. In addition, many researchers introduce N-GQDs to form photocatalysts with semiconductors for accelerating the separation of photogenerated carriers to achieve the purpose of enhancing photocatalytic activity, such as N-GQDs/BiOBr [6], Ag/N-GQDs/C3N4 [14], S, N-GQDs/TiO2 [15], and so on.

In this paper, N-GQDs/BiOX composites are synthesized by a simple solvothermal method and successfully achieve photodegradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. Comparing with pure BiOBr and BiOCl, the optical absorption range of N-GQDs/BiOX has a large red shift as well as lower photogenerated carrier recombination rate. This work suggests that N-GQDs dispersed on the surface of the three-dimensional hierarchical BiOX has a great potential application in degradation of organic contaminant.

N-GQDs were prepared according to the method reported by Sun [16]. 4 mmol of citric acid and 12 mmol of urea were dissolved in 20 ml of deionized water, stirred and clarified, and transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave kept at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was transferred to a large beaker, to which a large amount of anhydrous ethanol was added to purification, and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 6 min. Finally, the centrifuged sample was repeatedly centrifuged three times with absolute ethanol, and the obtained. The solid sample was dissolved in deionized water to obtain an aqueous N-GQDs solution.

N-GQDs/BiOX composites were synthesized by solvothermal method [17]. 2.25 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in a 15 mL solution mixed with both ethylene glycol and glacial acetic acid (volume ratio of 1:1) solution containing 70 u L N-GQDs. 2.25 mmol of NaX (X = Br, Cl) was dissolved in 15 mL of ethanol and deionized water (volume ratio of 1:1). After stirring for 30 min, the NaX solution was gradually added to the Bi(NO3)3·5H2O solution and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. The resulting solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave kept at 120 °C for 12 h and then cooled to room temperature. The sample was separated by centrifugation, washed several times with distilled water and ethanol, and dried at 80 °C to obtain 1 wt% N-GQDs/BiOX. The amount of NGQDs added was changed to 210, 350, 490, and 700 u L, which were sequentially named 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOX, 5 wt% N-GQDs/BiOX, 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOX, and 10 wt% N-GQDs/BiOX.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured on Germany Bruker AX in the range of 10–60°. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was investigated using Quanta 450 FEG of FEI Company of the USA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out on a PHI 5700 ESCA spectrometer utilizing Al HR radiation to analyze the elements on the surface of hybrid materials. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was measured on an Agilent carry 100. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was measured on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

In order to study the photocatalytic performance of composites with different N-GQDs content, RhB was selected as the target pollutant. 40 mg of catalyst was dispersed in 60 mL 12 mg/L RhB solution, first in the dark to stir for 60 min to achieve adsorption equilibrium, then photodegradation under 500 W Xenon lamp photolysis, take a sample every 15 min, centrifuge to take the supernatant through a UV spectrophotometer measured absorbance.

Fig. 3 SEM images of prepared photocatalysts. Fig. 2a–d, it can be seen that pure BiOBr and BiOCl are three-dimensional hierarchical flower-like microspheres structures composed of nanoplates with thicknesses of 20–40 nm and 30–60 nm. Fig. 2e–f show SEM images of 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl, showing that addition of N-GQDs has no effect on the shape of pure BiOX.

The elemental combinations and elemental oxidation states of N-GQDs/BiOX composites were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Through the full spectrum of the surface of the sample, it can be seen that there are Bi, O, Br, Cl and C elements (Fig. 3a–b). Due to the low amount of the N element compared with other elements, no signal of N element can be found in the survey XPS spectrum [6]. In Fig. 3c, the corresponding binding energy peaks for Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 are 164.9 and 159.6 eV, indicating the presence of Bi3+ in the composite. Fig. 3d shows peaks with binding energies of 529.8, 531 and 532.6 eV, which were assigned to Bi-O, O-H and C–O [5,18]. Fig. 3e shows peaks with binding energies of 68.4 and 69.3 eV, corresponded to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 for Br− in BiOBr [6]. N-GQDs/BiOCl shows peaks with binding energies of 198.4 and 200 eV, assigned to Cl 2P3/2 and Cl 2P1/2 for Cl− in BiOCl [19]. Fig. 3g shows peaks with binding energies of 284.6, 285.2, 286.6, 287.3 and 289.0 eV, which corresponded to C-C, C–N, C–O, C = O, and O-C = O, respectively [20,21]. Based on the above results, it was successfully confirmed that N-GQDs are loaded on the BiOX surface.

The BiOX, BiOCl, 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl samples are characterized by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS) at 200–800 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 that pure BiOCl has strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, but no absorption of visible light, while the absorption edge of pure BiOBr is around 420 nm. When BiOX composites a few N-GQDs, we can find that the absorption edge of the N-GQDs/BiOX has a clear red shift trend, of which the red shift of 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr is most obvious. BiOBr, BiOCl, 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl can also be obtained by the formula:
αhv = A(hv-Eg)n/2
In the formula, α is the absorption coefficient, h is the Planck constant, v is the optical frequency, A is the semiconductor material constant, Eg is the forbidden bandwidth band of the photocatalyst, and the value of n depends on the type of semiconductor [22]. Taking hv as the abscissa, (αhv)1/2 as the ordinate plots as follows:

The Eg values of BiOBr, BiOCl, 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl were determined to be 2.79, 3.39, 2.56 and 2.99 eV, respectively. It can be seen that a few N-GQDs can broaden the light absorption range of BiOX.

The PL spectrum shows the transfer and separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The N-GQDs show the strong photoluminescence under the maximum excitation wavelength at 360 nm in Fig. 6c. As shown in Fig. 6a–b, the BiOX has a strong emission peak at about 471 nm with the excitation wavelength at 315 nm [16]. The intensity of the emission peak become much lower upon introducing the N-GQDs into the BiOX, indicating that the N-GQDs introduction can effective separate photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOX showed the lowest PL intensity, indicating that the lowest recombination rate of photogenerated carriers.

With RhB as the target degradant, the adsorption effects and degradation efficiency of N-GQDs/BiOX composites with different mass fractions were studied under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7b and d, after stirring for 60 min in the dark, the adsorption rate of pure BiOBr and BiOCl was as high as 77.9% and 68.4%. After 105 min of light irradiation, the removal rate of RhB by BiOBr reached 89.3%. However, the pure BiOCl did not absorb in the visible light range, and the removal rate of RhB was 77.2%. When a few N-GQDs are added, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs are increased due to the broadening of the light absorption range. The degradation rate of RhB in composites increases with the increase of N-GQDs. From the Fig. 7a and c, RhB was almost removed after irradiating 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr for 60 min, while RhB could be degraded after 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOCl was irradiated for 75 min. When the addition of N-GQD exceeds 7%, the degradation efficiency of N-GQDs/BiOCl has a significant change, while N-GQDs/BiOBr has almost no change. Combined with experimental results, 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOX was determined as the optimal catalyst.

In order to study the possible reaction mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB, the active groups generated in the photocatalytic reaction of 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOX were studied by adding active group capture agents. The isopropanol (IPA), ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were added as hydroxyl radicals (
OH), photogenerated holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (
O2 −), respectively [23,24]. From the Fig. 8, when no capture agent was added, the degradation rate of 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr after 60 min of light irradiation was close to 100%. When the capture agent isopropanol was added, the removal time of RhB could be seen to be prolonged. When EDTA and BQ were added, there was still the presence of RhB after 105 min of light irradiation. The removal rates of were 90% and 83.5% respectively. The situation in Fig. 8b is similar to Fig. 8a. It can be seen from this that
O2- play a major role, and photogenerated h+ are only followed by
OH.

As shown in the Fig. 5
, the valence band (EVB) and conduction band (ECB) of pure BiOBr, BiOCl are calculated by the following formulas [25]: (Fig. 9 )
ECB=X-EC-0.5Eg
EVB=ECB+Eg

The X represents the absolute electric negative potential and Ec is the energy of free electrons on hydrogen scale. The EVB of BiOBr and BiOCl were calculated to be 3.35 and 3.85 eV, and the ECB were calculated to be 0.56 and 0.46 eV, respectively.

Under visible light irradiation, valence band electrons in BiOBr are excited to the conduction band. Due to the good electron capture ability of N-GQDs, electrons can be captured by N-GQD during the transfer process, and e− can reduce O2 on the surface of BiOBr to O2 −. In addition, N-GQDs can absorb light of longer wavelengths and convert light with shorter wavelengths into light to provide energy for BiOBr to photogenerated carriers. At the same time, RhB can generate electrons and RhB* under visible light irradiation, and electrons are quickly transferred to BiOBr. However, the BiOCl has a large band coefficient and does not absorb visible light, while the composite N-GQDs can provide energy for BiOCl generate electron-hole pairs for photodegrading RhB.

In summary, N-GQDs/BiOX composites were synthesized by a simple solvothermal method. The introduction of N-GQDs not only broadens the light absorption range of BiOX but also increases the photoelectron-hole pairs. 7%NGQDs/BiOX showed the highest degradation rate for RhB, the active species trapping experiments indicate that in the reaction process, the primary oxidative species are
O2 − and h+ for the degradation of RhB. This work provides evidence for improving the photocatalytic activity of semiconductor composite QDs in the future

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Education Department of Hebei Province. [No. ZD2017214]
